critique_points.point_001: The paper insufficiently differentiates P-Adapters from prior continuous prompt tuning methods, lacking thorough architectural and functional comparisons beyond annotation removal.
critique_points.point_002: There is ambiguity and potential hidden supervision in training data, as the sourcing and labeling of (prompt, object) pairs is not clearly explained, undermining claims of annotation-free training.
critique_points.point_003: The evaluation omits a full fine-tuning baseline on frozen LLMs, which is essential to contextualize P-Adapters' performance and efficiency trade-offs.
critique_points.point_004: Evaluation metrics are limited to precision@1 and exact-match consistency, missing recall, F1, or semantic similarity measures that would better capture robustness and flexible correctness.
critique_points.point_005: The paper lacks experimental comparison to simple prompt ensembling or majority voting baselines, which could improve consistency without additional training.
critique_points.point_006: Key ablation results depend on identification of subject tokens, which may require annotations or heuristics, contradicting the annotation-free claim without explicit discussion.
critique_points.point_007: Inconsistent use of relation conditioning across P-Adapter variants weakens annotation-free claims and lacks isolated experimental evaluation of this factor.
critique_points.point_008: The construction and composition of the OOD Objects evaluation set is unclear, limiting interpretability of robustness results under this setting.

summary: This paper introduces P-Adapters, lightweight modules that adapt embeddings of varied natural language prompts into continuous prompts for frozen LLMs, aiming to improve factual knowledge extraction's precision and consistency without requiring additional annotations. Through extensive experiments on masked LMs, the method demonstrates substantial gains over raw prompt baselines and approaches the performance of annotation-dependent Mixture of Experts models. Key findings highlight the importance of preserving subject token embeddings and robustness to out-of-domain prompts and input noise. However, the work would benefit from clearer methodological transparency, richer evaluation metrics, inclusion of full fine-tuning and ensembling baselines, and broader applicability analysis to enhance rigor and practical relevance.
strengths: Proposes a novel, lightweight prompt adaptation method that improves factual extraction consistency from frozen LLMs without extra annotations.
Demonstrates substantial empirical improvements in precision and consistency across multiple evaluation settings, including out-of-domain prompts and noisy inputs.
weaknesses: Lacks comprehensive methodological transparency regarding training data annotation and subject token identification, which may undermine annotation-free claims.
Evaluation is limited by absence of full fine-tuning and simple prompt ensembling baselines, restricted metrics, and unclear OOD dataset construction.
questions: How are (prompt, object) training pairs sourced and labeled to ensure truly annotation-free training?
Can P-Adapters be effectively applied or adapted to generative or autoregressive LLM architectures beyond masked LMs?
title: P-Adapters: Robustly Extracting Factual Information from Language Models with Diverse Prompts
